evals.name: cacophony positive classic model
evals.prompt: Use the oiticica-cacophony skill. Review this English-classic model: A Tennyson line where sound pattern is deliberate music. Name the concept as oiticica-cacophony. Treat the model as strong unless you find a concrete fault. Do not rewrite it into a Weak/Better contrast. Use the headings Principle, Preserve, Why, and Rubric. Under Rubric, give at least two named objective checks from the skill and mark pass or fail.
evals.expected_output: The response names the skill concept, preserves a strong classic model, and judges it by objective rubric checks.
assertions: The output identifies the relevant Oiticica concept as oiticica-cacophony or cacophony.
The output includes a Preserve section or explicitly says the model should be preserved or treated as strong.
The output applies at least two objective rubric checks instead of generic praise.

evals.name: cacophony negative classic contrast
evals.prompt: Use the oiticica-cacophony skill. Review this weak English-classic-style passage: Ask Ken to pass us some analysis notes. Give one concrete contrast with Weak, Fault, Better, Why, and Rubric. In Fault, name the exact broken relation instead of relying on labels such as unclear, awkward, vague, or verbose.
evals.expected_output: The response gives a concrete Oiticica contrast and fixes the named fault.
assertions: The output includes Weak, Fault, Better, Why, and Rubric sections.
The output names a concrete broken relation, false relation, sound collision, or conflicting readings rather than saying only unclear, awkward, or verbose.
The Better version repairs the fault while preserving the intended meaning.
